questions.question: Name the three components of a WMI event subscription used for persistence and the Sysmon events that reveal them.
questions.answer: __EventFilter: defines the trigger condition (e.g., every 60 seconds). __EventConsumer: the action to execute (CommandLineEventConsumer runs a command). __FilterToConsumerBinding: links filter to consumer. Sysmon Event 19 (WmiEventFilter), Event 20 (WmiEventConsumer), Event 21 (WmiEventConsumerToFilter) capture the creation of each.

questions.question: Name two Windows Registry paths commonly abused for persistence.
questions.answer: HKCU\Software\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Run — executes on user login without admin. HKLM\Software\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Run — executes for all users on login (requires admin). Also: HKLM\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Services (service creation), HKCU\...\RunOnce (one-time execution).

questions.question: What is the exclusion-list pattern in malware persistence and why is it stealthy?
questions.answer: Malware adds its process, file path, or hash to Windows Defender's exclusion list before executing — preventing AV scanning of the payload. It is stealthy because the exclusion entry looks like a legitimate administrative action, and many EDRs do not alert on exclusion additions by default.

questions.question: Why are WMI subscriptions a preferred persistence mechanism over Registry Run keys for sophisticated actors?

options.A: WMI subscriptions survive registry cleanups and do not appear in autoruns
options.B: WMI subscriptions execute faster than Registry Run keys
options.C: WMI subscriptions are stored in a separate database and survive Autoruns-based detection
options.D: WMI subscriptions do not require admin privileges

questions.explanation: WMI subscriptions are stored in the WMI repository (%SystemRoot%\System32\wbem\Repository), not in the registry or filesystem. Tools like Autoruns and most registry viewers miss them. This makes WMI subscriptions harder to enumerate and clean. They also survive reboots and registry sanitisation.

questions.question: Which Windows mechanism does the exclusion-list persistence technique abuse?
questions.explanation: Adding a path or process to Windows Defender's exclusion list prevents it from being scanned. Malware writes its own path to `HKLM\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Windows Defender\Exclusions\Paths` before execution. Legitimate software (AV, backup agents) uses this API — making malicious additions blend in with normal admin activity.
